focus: Resolve Q5 source-of-truth and migration policy: authority boundaries, staged bootstrap/shadow reads, parity gates, cutover, rollback, repair/rebuild, deletion, and operator workflows.
noveltyJustification: 5 of 8 findings were fully new and 3 partially new; resolving Q5 earned the 0.10 simplicity bonus (0.8125 + 0.10, rounded to 0.91).
ruledOut: SQLite as independently editable style-content authority
Permanent dual reads or corpus-walk fallback
Blind checkpoint restore as final state

summary: Flat artifacts remain content authority while the published SQLite generation becomes sole normal-query authority; the same content is never dual-authoritative.
Migration uses baseline, staged bootstrap, shadow dual reads, canary, query cutover, then retirement of legacy query scanning.
Parity separates exact census/eligibility/legacy compatibility from native-v2 relevance and operational metrics.
Cutover requires source-bound reports plus parity, health, latency, tombstone, rebuild, restore, and rollback drills.
Rollback reverses an adapter or generation pointer and never writes derived database rows back over authoritative files.
Bounded derived repair is distinct from staged full rebuild; checkpoint restore must reindex newer files and preserve a rebuild sentinel until healthy.
Deletes originate in the source plane, pass through confirmed tombstones, and hard-purge only after retention, checkpoint, and parity gates.
Operators need explicit status/bootstrap/sync/shadow/checkpoint/cutover/rollback/repair/rebuild/tombstone workflows; flat files retire from query input but not current hydration.
No database generation may be promoted unless its recorded source aggregate identifies the verified authoritative flat-file snapshot used to build it.

reason: No deterministic conflict winner and current hydration verifies live file bytes.

summary: Q5 is answered, but max-iterations stop policy requires iterations 7-10 regardless of the 0.91 novelty telemetry.
